title: Snapshot Git Status Before Recovery
hook: Before rollback commands, capture the branch and dirty files.
problem: An incident starts with an unknown working tree, and you need to see whether local edits could be overwritten by recovery work.
starting_state: A disposable demo repository has release tags, one suspect release, and one uncommitted local edit.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Git prints the current branch and working-tree state.
when_to_use: Use before reset, restore, checkout, revert, or rollback work so local edits are visible.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat status alone as a release audit; inspect the commit history and deployment pointer too.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A short branch line for main and a modified app/config.yml entry.

shorts.title: Check git status before rollback.
shorts.voiceover: Before recovery commands, run a status snapshot. Dirty files are the easiest thing to overwrite by accident.
shorts.caption: Status first, rollback second.

linkedin.question: Do you snapshot git status before incident rollback work?

youtube.description: Use git status to inspect branch and dirty files before recovery or rollback commands.

title: Map Recent Release Commits
hook: A rollback is easier when the last few release tags are visible.
problem: You need to see which tagged release commits exist before choosing a rollback target.
starting_state: A Git repository contains annotated release tags for recent deploys.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Git prints the recent commit graph with tags.
when_to_use: Use during rollback planning when you need the shape of recent releases quickly.
when_not_to_use: Do not rely on commit order alone when your deploy system can point at a different artifact or symlink.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A compact commit graph showing release-2026-06-25-1030 and earlier release tags.

shorts.title: Map release history fast.
shorts.voiceover: Show the recent graph with decorations. The tags tell you which commits are rollback candidates.
shorts.caption: Use the commit graph before choosing.

linkedin.question: What command do you use to find the rollback target commit?
linkedin.cta: Add tag visibility to incident triage.

youtube.description: Display recent release history with git log decorations before rollback planning.

experiments.variant_a: A rollback target should be visible.
experiments.variant_b: Do not choose rollback commits blind.

title: Show Files Changed Since Last Good Release
hook: Compare the suspect release against the last known-good tag.
problem: A new release is suspect, and you need a quick list of files changed after the last stable release.
starting_state: HEAD is the suspect release and release-2026-06-25-1000 is the previous stable tag.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Git lists files changed between the previous release tag and HEAD.
when_to_use: Use when deciding whether a rollback is likely to touch code, config, migrations, or release metadata.
when_not_to_use: Do not use this as proof of root cause; it only narrows the changed surface.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A name-status list including app/config.yml, db/migrate.sql, public/index.html, and release metadata.

shorts.title: What changed since good?
shorts.voiceover: Diff the suspect release against the last known-good tag. Now the rollback discussion has a file list.
shorts.caption: Compare against last good.

linkedin.question: During rollback triage, do you inspect changed files before reverting?

youtube.description: Use git diff name-status to compare a suspect release against the last known-good tag.

experiments.variant_b: Rollback scope starts with changed files.

title: Find a Discarded Commit in Reflog
hook: A reset does not mean the commit vanished.
problem: Someone ran reset during incident work and you need to locate the commit that disappeared from the branch tip.
starting_state: The fixture created an incident note commit, then reset the branch back one commit.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Git prints recent HEAD movements from the reflog.
when_to_use: Use after an accidental reset, checkout, commit amend, or branch movement.
when_not_to_use: Do not wait weeks to inspect reflog; entries can expire or be pruned.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Recent reflog entries showing a reset and the discarded incident-note commit.

shorts.title: Reset is not always lost.
shorts.voiceover: After a reset, check the reflog. The old tip is usually still there long enough to recover.
shorts.caption: Use reflog for lost commits.

linkedin.question: Have you recovered a commit from reflog during an incident?

youtube.description: Use git reflog to locate a commit discarded by reset.

experiments.variant_a: A reset does not erase the commit immediately.
experiments.variant_b: Find the old tip in reflog.

title: Branch a Recovered Commit
hook: Put a name on the reflog commit before it slips away.
problem: You found a useful discarded commit in reflog and need to preserve it with a branch name.
starting_state: HEAD@{1} points to a discarded incident note commit in the fixture reflog.
what_changed: A local branch named recovered-incident-note is created inside the disposable demo repository.
when_to_use: Use when a reflog entry contains work you may need to inspect, cherry-pick, or restore later.
when_not_to_use: Do not create branches from reflog entries you have not inspected if the repository contains sensitive or unrelated work.
undo: Delete the local branch with git branch -D recovered-incident-note inside the demo repo.
expected_output: The new branch points at the recovered incident note commit.

shorts.title: Save the reflog commit.
shorts.voiceover: Once you find the lost commit, branch it. A branch name is easier to review than a temporary reflog selector.
shorts.caption: Name recovered work quickly.

linkedin.question: When you find lost work in reflog, do you branch it immediately?
linkedin.cta: Turn temporary recovery into a named ref.

youtube.description: Create a local branch from a reflog entry to preserve recovered work.

experiments.variant_a: Put a name on recovered work.
experiments.variant_b: Reflog finds it, branch preserves it.

title: Restore One File From Last Good Release
hook: Recover a config file without rolling back the whole branch.
problem: One file is dirty or suspect, and you want to restore just that path from a known-good release tag.
starting_state: app/config.yml has an uncommitted debug edit and differs from the last stable release tag.
what_changed: app/config.yml in the disposable repo is replaced with the version from release-2026-06-25-1000.
when_to_use: Use when you need a targeted file recovery from a tag, commit, or branch.
when_not_to_use: Do not run this on a path with uncommitted work you still need unless you have saved or reviewed the diff.
undo: Restore the file again from HEAD or discard the disposable fixture copy.
expected_output: The diff for app/config.yml changes after the targeted restore.

shorts.title: Restore one file from a tag.
shorts.voiceover: You do not always need a full rollback. Restore one known-good file from the release tag and review the diff.
shorts.caption: Targeted restore beats panic reset.

linkedin.question: Do you use targeted file restores during release incidents?

youtube.description: Use git restore --source to recover one file from a known-good release tag.

experiments.variant_a: Restore one file, not the whole repo.

title: Check the Active Release Symlink
hook: Git may say one thing while the release pointer serves another.
problem: A deployment uses release directories and a current symlink, and you need to confirm which release is active.
starting_state: The fixture has release directories and releases/current points at the suspect 10:30 release.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The symlink target and release metadata are printed.
when_to_use: Use during deploy or rollback triage when production is selected by a filesystem pointer.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume Git HEAD and the active release symlink always match.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: The current symlink target and VERSION file for release 2026-06-25-1030.

shorts.title: Which release is active?
shorts.voiceover: If deploys use a current symlink, inspect that pointer directly. Git history is only part of the incident.
shorts.caption: Check the release pointer.

linkedin.question: Does your rollback checklist verify the active release pointer?
linkedin.cta: Check deploy state outside Git too.

youtube.description: Inspect a release-directory current symlink during rollback triage.

experiments.variant_a: Git is not the only deployment state.

title: Rollback a Release Symlink in a Sandbox
hook: Practice the pointer switch where the blast radius is zero.
problem: You need to rehearse a symlink-style rollback by pointing current at the previous stable release directory.
starting_state: releases/current points at the suspect 2026-06-25-1030 release inside a disposable fixture.
what_changed: The releases/current symlink inside the disposable fixture points to 2026-06-25-1000.
when_to_use: Use in a sandbox or controlled deploy path when rollback is implemented by moving a current symlink.
when_not_to_use: Do not run this against a real production releases directory unless your deployment process explicitly calls for it.
undo: Point releases/current back to 2026-06-25-1030 or recreate the fixture.
expected_output: The current symlink resolves to 2026-06-25-1000 and its VERSION status is stable.

shorts.title: Rollback the symlink safely.
shorts.voiceover: In this sandbox, rollback is just moving current to the last stable release. Practice it before production pressure.
shorts.caption: Symlink rollbacks need verification.

linkedin.question: Have you rehearsed your symlink rollback command outside production?
linkedin.cta: Practice rollback where mistakes are cheap.

youtube.description: Practice a release-directory symlink rollback in a disposable fixture.

experiments.variant_b: Move current only when you know the target.

title: Preview the Patch a Rollback Would Apply
hook: Show the exact file changes before moving the branch back.
problem: You know the last good release tag, but you want to inspect what a rollback would remove before changing anything.
starting_state: HEAD is the suspect release and release-2026-06-25-1000 is the previous stable tag.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Git prints the patch summary from the current commit back to the last good release.
when_to_use: Use before reset, revert, or deploy rollback when you need a quick preview of the files and line counts involved.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat a small diff as automatically safe; migrations, config, and external state still need review.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A diffstat showing the config, migration, public page, and release metadata changes that rollback would remove.

shorts.title: Preview rollback first.
shorts.voiceover: Before moving a branch or release pointer, diff current against the last good tag. The rollback patch should be visible.
shorts.caption: Rollback changes should not be a surprise.

linkedin.question: Do you preview rollback patches before changing refs?
linkedin.cta: Make the rollback diff part of incident review.

youtube.description: Use git diff against a last-good tag to preview the patch a rollback would apply.

experiments.variant_b: The rollback patch should be visible.

title: Revert the Suspect Release Commit
hook: Undo a bad release with a new commit instead of rewriting history.
problem: The suspect release has already been shared, and you need a rollback commit that preserves branch history.
command: cd /lab/git-recovery-rollback && git restore -- app/config.yml && git revert --no-edit release-2026-06-25-1030
starting_state: The fixture branch contains a tagged suspect release commit and one dirty config line in the disposable repo.
what_changed: The dirty config line is discarded, then a new local revert commit is created inside the disposable demo repository.
when_to_use: Use when a bad commit is already published or shared and rollback should be represented as a forward-moving commit.
when_not_to_use: Do not use revert blindly when the bad release included database migrations or external side effects that need a separate rollback plan.
undo: Reset the disposable fixture branch back one commit or recreate the fixture.
expected_output: Git restores the dirty file, creates a revert commit, and the recent log shows the new Revert commit above the suspect release.

commands: cd /lab/git-recovery-rollback && git restore -- app/config.yml && git revert --no-edit release-2026-06-25-1030

shorts.title: Revert, do not rewrite.
shorts.voiceover: If the bad release commit is already shared, create a revert commit. History keeps moving forward and the rollback is reviewable.
shorts.caption: Use revert for shared history.

linkedin.question: When do you prefer git revert over resetting a release branch?
linkedin.cta: Use forward-moving rollback commits for shared history.

youtube.description: Use git revert to create a reviewable rollback commit for a suspect release.
